burial.plot_notes: St Jude's Cemetery Oakville Halton ON.

parents.father_research_notes: Henry Bath of Longlands (Swansea Wales, d. Oct 13, 1864) — prominent Welsh social and business leader; died of heart attack 6 months after Percy's birth. Bath family ran Henry Bath & Son. Buried in Quaker meeting house family vault Swansea despite later Anglican family identity.
parents.mother_research_notes: Marian Osler Bath (b.1841 d.?, 21 at marriage to 64-year-old Henry of Longlands Nov 14, 1862) — daughter of physician-turned-author Edward Osler; her family the Oslers were staunch Anglican. The Marian Osler→Percy Atkinson lineage gave a connection to the Sir William Osler Canadian medical dynasty.

career.notes: Acquired interest in genealogy and compiled the Bath family tree. Original copy at Hamilton Public Library.

burial.plot_notes: St Jude's Cemetery, Oakville Halton ON — with husband Percy and children.

narrative_blocks.body: Alice Ruth Whitney married Percy Atkinson Bath April 28, 1886 in Montreal and moved with him to Toronto, then to Oakville Ontario where Percy purchased the lakefront home 'Belaire' built 1856 on Lake Ontario. They raised three sons and a daughter at Belaire and attended St James Anglican Church in town. Two of their sons would serve with the Canadian Forces in World War I — including Captain Edward 'Tod' Osler Bath who was captured at the front in 1915 and died of influenza in London 1918 after release.

sources.label: Canadian Illustrated News of Montreal, 7 July 1883 obituary

parents.father_research_notes: Family was United Empire Loyalist stock per the 1883 Canadian Illustrated News obituary — refugees from the American Revolution who fled north to British Canada. Not built per Jalapeño Rule.

notable.notable_blurb: Montreal merchant, Telegraph Co founder, Horticultural Society president

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Nathaniel Stevens Whitney (1820-1883), Montreal merchant and civic leader.

quotes.text: Another of Montreal's worthies, the esteemed and widely-beloved gentleman, Nathaniel S. Whitney, has at last gone over to the majority at the comparatively early age of 62 years. Respected by all alike, for his loving and excellent qualities, he will long be missed, and his death leaves a blank in a large circle which will not soon be filled up.
quotes.source: Canadian Illustrated News of Montreal, July 7, 1883
quotes.notes: Obituary excerpt for Nathaniel Stevens Whitney.

military_service.notes: Served in both winters of the Rebellions of 1837-1838 (Lower Canada Rebellion), although not of the age required by law (he was only 17 in 1837). Per the Canadian Illustrated News: 'showed himself a worthy descendant [of United Empire Loyalist stock] by serving in both winters of the rebellion, although not of the age required by law.'

burial.plot_notes: Cimetière Mont-Royal Montreal — Mr. Whitney was himself a trustee of the cemetery.

career.notes: Entered wholesale dry goods business after moving to Montreal at an early age from Frelighsburg.
Subsequently entered the leather business, in which he continued until his death.
The first meeting of the Montreal Telegraph Company at its organization was held in his office — placing him at the founding of one of the earliest Canadian telecommunications enterprises.
Largely instrumental in forming the Montreal Horticultural Society, of which he was latterly president.
Life Governor 'not in name only, but in deeds and activity, as the annual reports have testified' per the Canadian Illustrated News obituary.
Trustee of the Mount Royal Cemetery — where he himself would be buried in 1883.

career.role: Lay delegate to Synod / Governor of Diocesan College
career.organization: Church of England (Anglican Church of Canada)
career.notes: Devoted adherent of the Church of England. Lay delegate to the Synod for many years; member of the executive committee; Governor of the Diocesan College.

narrative_blocks.header: Telegraph Company founded in his Montreal office
narrative_blocks.body: Nathaniel Stevens Whitney came to Montreal at an early age from Frelighsburg Quebec and entered the wholesale dry goods business, subsequently the leather trade, which he continued until his death. The first meeting of the Montreal Telegraph Company at its organization was held in his office — placing him at the founding of one of the earliest Canadian telecommunications enterprises. He was instrumental in founding the Montreal Horticultural Society and served as its latter-day president.

narrative_blocks.header: Life governor MGH, Mount Royal Cemetery trustee
narrative_blocks.body: Whitney was Life Governor of the Montreal General Hospital — not in name only but in deeds and activity, as the annual reports testified — and a trustee of Mount Royal Cemetery, where he himself would eventually be buried. A devoted Anglican, he served for many years as lay delegate to the Synod, executive committee member, and Governor of the Diocesan College. Several times invited to stand as Liberal candidate for his native county, he always declined.

Of United Empire Loyalist stock, Whitney served in both winters of the Rebellions of 1837-1838 at Lower Canada — although he was only seventeen in 1837 and not of the age required by law. He caught the cold resulting in his death while visiting The Hills, his country residence at Frelighsburg where he kept a large stock farm. The Canadian Illustrated News obituary of July 7, 1883 called him 'another of Montreal's worthies, the esteemed and widely-beloved gentleman.'

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Hannah Jane Mills Whitney (1826-1905).

burialCemetery.name: St Jude's Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: St Jude's Cemetery, Oakville Halton Regional Municipality Ontario. Burial site of the Bath family — Alice Ruth Whitney Bath (Hooker descendant) and her husband Percy Atkinson Bath, along with their children including WWI POW Capt. Edward Osler 'Tod' Bath.
